evidence: The paper uses MEIP (Mean Error Increase When Permuted), an unconditional permutation-based importance method, not impurity-based importance. Permutation importance is robust to the magnification and ranking biases of Gini/MDI. Although the paper interprets magnitude/ranking ("soil and climatic properties were the most important factors"), conclusions are substantiated by partial dependence plots showing specific optimal ranges, k-fold cross-validation, and Log-Response Ratio for effect direction. No impurity bias applies here.
